revision.text: #REDIRECT [[三省六部]]

'''六部''' *[[三省六部]]（さんしょうりくぶ） - 中国隋代から清代まで行われた政治制度。 *[[六十六部]]（ろくじゅうろくぶ） - 六十六部の法華経を六十六か所の霊地に納めるために巡礼した僧。六部僧ともいう。
{{aimai}}

'''六部''' *[[三省六部]]（さんしょうりくぶ） - 中国隋代から清代まで行われた政治制度。 *[[六十六部]]（ろくじゅうろくぶ） - 六十六部の法華経を六十六か所の霊地に納めるために白衣に手甲、脚絆姿で巡礼した僧。六部僧ともいう。
{{aimai}}

revision.text:
'''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}} || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

== References == {{Reflist}}

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|ja|六部}}''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan}}), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制}}), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}, it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|ja|官僚}}. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|ja|唐}} Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan}}), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制}}, {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei}}), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|ja|流内官}} (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|ja|尚書省}} || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|ja|尚書令}} || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|ja|門下省}} || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} and {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}, it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官}} (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官}} (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官}} (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典}} (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table}}
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, positions like {{lang-ja|令}} (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐}} (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[category:new pages]][[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:New pages]] to [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗]] {{Source Wikipedia]] [[Category:Four-Tier Officials forks]]

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

forks]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗]] {{Source Wikipedia]] [[Category:Four-Tier Officials forks]]

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

== References == {{Reflist]]
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]] * {{ill|Japanese government structure|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:日本の官制]] * {{ill|Rank and Title in Japan|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官位]] * {{ill|Four-Rank Bureaucracy in China|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:四等官]] * {{ill|Military ranks and insignia|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:軍隊における階級呼称一覧]]

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).

Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."

pages]] [[Category:Four-Tier Officials]][[ja:四等官]] [[de:Shitōkan]] ]] [[Category:Old Onmyoji forks]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗]] {{Source Wikipedia]] [[Category:Four-Tier Officials forks]] [[Category:kk]][[ceb:Six Ministries]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]]

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

[[Category:Four-Tier Officials forks]] [[Category:kk]][[ceb:Six Ministries]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]]

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

[[Category:kk]][[ceb:Six Ministries]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Six Ministries]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Six Ministries]][[Category:qq]]

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

revision.text:
{| class="wikitable" |- | '''{{ill|Six Ministries|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:六部]]''' |} '''Four-Rank System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Shitōkan, Historical kana usage: Shitōkwan]]), or the '''Four-Rank Official System''' ({{lang-ja|四等官制]]), refers to the hierarchical organization of key officials within each bureau under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令制]]. Originating in China's {{ill|Ritsuryō|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:律令]], it served as the foundation of an elaborate {{ill|bureaucratic system|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:官僚]]. Japan adopted the Four-Rank System alongside the implementation of the Ritsuryō system.
== Overview ==
The Four-Rank System consists of four grades: '''chief officer''', '''deputy officer''', '''adjudicating officer''', and '''administrative officer'''. Their respective duties were outlined in the Ritsuryō codes.
== The Four-Rank System in Tang China ==
{{ill|Tang dynasty|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:唐]] Ritsuryō administration featured a hierarchy with chief officers, assistant adjudicators, adjudicators, and administrative officers. Additional roles, such as inspectors ({{lang-ja|検勾官]], {{Transliteration|rom=Kenkyūkan]]), ensured accountability. While central offices had diverse titles, regional offices adopted more standardized terminology.
In Tang law: - Fourth-rank officers (administrative) managed clerical tasks and archives without influencing decisions. - Adjudicators (third rank) held decision-making powers and forwarded cases to deputy officers. - Deputy officers (second rank) reviewed and finalized decisions. - Chief officers conducted the ultimate review.
This process, termed the "Three-Adjudication System" ({{lang-ja|三判制]], {{Transliteration|rom=Sanpansei]]), emphasized hierarchical reviews.<ref>Naito Kenkichi, "Study of Tang Dynasty Official Documents," *Chinese Legal History Research*, Yuhikaku, 1963.</ref>
{{ill|Inner-stream officials|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:流内官]] (chief to adjudicators) differed significantly in rank and status from outer-stream officials (administrative).
; Tang Four-Rank Table {| class="wikitable" |- ! Institution !! Chief Officer !! Deputy Officer !! Adjudicating Officer !! Inspector !! Administrative Officer |- | {{ill|Shangshu Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書省]] || {{ill|Shangshu Ling|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:尚書令]] || Pushe || Cheng || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | || Shangshu || Shilang || Langzhong || Langzhong || Zhushi |- | {{ill|Menxia Sheng|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:門下省]] || Shizhong || Huangmen Shilang || Jishi Zhong || Luzhi || Zhushi |}
== The Four-Rank System in Japan ==
Japan introduced the Four-Rank System during the 7th century based on Tang models. Under the {{ill|Taihō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:大宝律令]] and {{ill|Yōrō Code|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:養老律令]], it established four roles: {{lang-ja|長官]] (chief officer), {{lang-ja|次官]] (deputy officer), {{lang-ja|判官]] (adjudicator), and {{lang-ja|主典]] (administrator). Unlike Tang practices, Japan did not adopt the "Three-Adjudication System."
{{Four-Rank Table]]
== Four-Rank System in Silla ==
In {{ill|Silla|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:新羅]], positions like {{lang-ja|令]] (chief) and {{lang-ja|佐]] (assistant) existed, though exact correlations remain speculative due to scarce records.
== Other Applications ==
During the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Six Ministries-qq]] [[ja:明治維新]], Japan revived the Four-Rank framework under restorationist policies. However, modernized names and roles reflected unique adaptations.

* '''Rikubu''' (りくぶ) – A general term for the six government ministries that managed administration in the central government of China from the Sui to Qing dynasties. ⇒ {{ill|Three Departments and Six Ministries|ja|三省六部}} * '''Rokubu''' (ろくぶ) – An abbreviation for {{ill|Sixty-Six Pilgrimage Monks|ja|六十六部}}, referring to itinerant Buddhist monks on pilgrimage.
{{aimai}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六部]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六部]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11392575]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Rikubu / Rokubu''' (六部)
* '''Rikubu''' (りくぶ) – A general term for the six government ministries that managed administration in the central government of China from the Sui to Qing dynasties. ⇒ [[:en:Three Departments and Six Ministries|Three Departments and Six Ministries]] * '''Rokubu''' (ろくぶ) – An abbreviation for {{ill|Sixty-Six Pilgrimage Monks|ja|六十六部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixty-Six Pilgrimage Monks|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本廻国大乗妙典六十六部経聖}}, referring to itinerant Buddhist monks on pilgrimage.
{{disambiguation}}
